topic_description: Evaluation test suites for LLM applications — prompts, agents, RAG pipelines — covering dataset design, scoring methods (exact match, rubric-based, LLM-as-judge), regression tracking, and cost/latency instrumentation.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

A good LLM evaluation suite is a product-quality test system, not a demo. Its job is to measure whether your LLM application (prompts, agents, RAG pipelines) delivers the intended behavior reliably, safely, and efficiently—now and as it evolves. That means clear definitions of “good,” representative and reproducible datasets, valid scoring, believable baselines, regression tracking, and observable cost/latency. Below are the key principles and trade-offs.

- Readability and Maintainability
  - Clarity beats cleverness: Tests should be easy to read and modify by someone new to the project. Human-readable case files (YAML/JSONL), concise naming, and documented rubrics make refactors safe and intentional.
  - Modularization prevents rot: Separate datasets from scoring logic and from harness plumbing. Pin versions of datasets, prompts, and models. Tag tests by capability and scenario to keep scope comprehensible.
  - Change control matters: Track when/why a test changes, how labels were produced, and what baseline it replaces. Prevent “quiet” drift of ground truth or rubrics.

- Correctness and Validity
  - Measure the right thing: Use exact match and structural validation where the output has an objective format (JSON, SQL, code, entities); use rubric-based or LLM-as-judge when semantics matter.
  - Bias and leakage control: Avoid judges that see ground truth if not needed; randomize candidate order to reduce position bias; ensure judge prompts don’t leak private data.
  - Reliability and power: Reduce noise with deterministic settings where possible, repeated trials for stochastic systems, bootstrap confidence intervals, and predefined effect-size thresholds to gate changes.

- Safety
  - Treat safety like any other requirement: Include jailbreak/prompt-injection tests, PII exfiltration checks, hallucination/overclaiming tests, and content safety thresholds in every suite.
  - Test the whole chain: Safety issues often arise from retrieval, tool use, or formatting steps; evaluate end-to-end behavior and component-level guardrails.

- Performance and Cost
  - Costs are part of quality: Token spend and latency are first-class metrics; record them per test and per tag. Track p50/p95/p99 latencies and amortized cost per task.
  - Avoid benchmarking illusions: Warm caches and retries can mask real latency; separate cold-start and warm-path measurements and log both.

- Common Failure Modes
  - Flaky tests from nondeterminism: Unpinned params, changing external tools, or dynamic URLs produce noise; control seeds, mock or record-replay tools, and declare xfail for known flaky cases while fixing root causes.
  - Overfitting to the eval: Narrow, repeated fine-tuning on a single suite yields cosmetically improved scores without generalization; maintain holdout sets, rotate adversarial sets, and track distribution shift.
  - Mis-specified ground truth: Poor labels or brittle exact-match expectations will mislead; include tolerance, normalization, and human-reviewed anchor sets.
  - Misused LLM-as-judge: Uncalibrated judges can encode model or vendor bias; validate judges against gold labels and monitor drift.
  - Missing regression context: Without baselines, confidence intervals, and acceptance thresholds, teams overreact to noise and ship regressions.

- Contested Areas (reasonable practitioners disagree)
  - Temperature during eval: Some insist on temperature=0 for determinism; others sample multiple outputs to reflect real use and reduce variance.
  - LLM-as-judge as primary metric: Some rely on it for semantic fidelity; others restrict to it only after validating against human gold standards.
  - Chain-of-thought visibility: Some judges request rationales for better signal; others ban CoT to avoid leakage, bias, and reproducibility issues.
  - Pairwise preference vs scalar scoring: Pairwise can be more discriminative but is more complex to operationalize.
  - Synthetic data: Acceptable for breadth and adversarial coverage, but opinions differ on how much synthetic data should influence gating.

In practice, strong suites do the following: they stratify datasets by capability and difficulty; log inputs, outputs, tokens, latency, and environment; use exact/structural checks by default and only rely on judges where necessary; calibrate judges and compute error bars; run in CI with gates on quality, safety, and cost; and keep tests stable through versioning, seeds, and mocks—without losing realism.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

Preamble
- Scope: This rules file defines how to design, implement, and operate evaluation suites for LLM applications (prompts, agents, RAG pipelines), including datasets, scoring, regression tracking, safety, and cost/latency instrumentation.
- Audience: Engineers, data scientists, and AI tooling who build, run, and gate releases with LLM evals.

Structure
- Separate datasets, scoring logic, and execution harness into distinct modules. — Isolation lowers coupling and reduces accidental drift.
- Store test cases as human-readable JSONL/YAML with one case per record. — Flat, greppable files are easy to diff and audit.
- Tag each test with capability, domain, difficulty, and safety flags. — Tags enable targeted runs and stratified reporting.
- Version datasets, prompts, and configurations explicitly. — Versioning prevents silent changes from invalidating baselines.
- Keep tests hermetic by default; mock or record–replay external tools. — Hermeticity reduces flakiness from network and time variance.
- Use stable identifiers for tests and do not recycle IDs. — Persistent IDs ensure longitudinal tracking survives edits.
- Provide a minimal README per dataset describing sourcing, labeling, and known limits. — Provenance increases trust and maintainability.
- Define skip and xfail semantics and document them in code. — Explicit control prevents accidental green runs.

Datasets
- Prefer small human-labeled anchor sets plus larger synthetic/adversarial sets. — Anchors ensure validity; synthetic expands coverage.
- Keep a private holdout set not used for model iteration. — Holdouts detect overfitting to the public suite.
- Stratify datasets by scenario and difficulty; report metrics per stratum. — Stratification exposes where regressions actually occur.
- Include negative, edge, and adversarial cases for each capability. — Robustness is measured at the edges, not the median.
- Deduplicate and decontaminate test content against training and docs. — Leakage makes scores meaningless.
- Document label quality, inter-annotator agreement, and sampling method. — Label transparency guards against hidden bias.
- Limit per-batch prompt examples to avoid context contamination. — Cross-case leakage corrupts signals.
- Cap dataset growth; add new files instead of mutating old ones. — Append-only datasets preserve history and reproducibility.

Scoring (Exact/Structural)
- Default to exact or structural checks when the task has objective outputs. — Objective checks are robust and cheap.
- Normalize strings (case, whitespace, punctuation, Unicode) before matching. — Normalization removes irrelevant variance.
- Validate JSON/XML outputs against schemas and reject malformed structures. — Structure must be correct before semantics.
- Score code by executing in a sandbox with time/memory limits. — Execution reveals functional correctness beyond surface form.
- Use span-level F1/EM for extractive QA and set clear tie-breaking rules. — Standard metrics enable comparability.
- Treat formatting/units as part of correctness only when requirements demand it. — Overly strict checks create false negatives.

Scoring (Rubric-Based and Heuristic)
- Write rubrics as checklists with binary or scaled criteria and weights. — Structured rubrics reduce grader ambiguity.
- Compute both overall and per-criterion scores and log failures by criterion. — Granular feedback accelerates debugging.
- Use regex and heuristic validation for domain constraints (e.g., SQL syntax, date ranges). — Cheap heuristics catch common errors fast.
- For long-form tasks, use recall-oriented and precision-oriented sub-scores. — Balanced metrics discourage verbosity or omission.

LLM-as-Judge
- Use LLM-as-judge only after validating against a human-labeled gold set. — Calibration controls judge bias.
- Blind the judge to the ground truth unless explicitly comparing candidates. — Blinding reduces leakage and anchoring.
- Randomize candidate order and equilibrate prompts across arms. — Randomization mitigates position bias.
- Constrain judge output to a strict JSON schema with label and reasons. — Structured judgments are machine-checkable.
- Compute inter-judge agreement by sampling multiple judges or seeds. — Agreement quantifies reliability.
- Revalidate judges after model or vendor changes. — Judge drift is real and silent.
- Prefer pairwise comparisons for subtle quality differences. — Pairwise judgments are more discriminative at the margin. (contested)
- Disallow chain-of-thought in judge prompts and collect reasons as bullet points only. — CoT can leak, bias, and harm reproducibility. (contested)

Determinism and Reproducibility
- Pin model versions, temperatures, seeds, and decoding params in config. — Reproducibility demands immutable knobs.
- Set temperature=0 for eval unless simulating production sampling. — Determinism reduces test noise. (contested)
- If production samples, run k≥3 generations per case and score by majority or best-of defined in advance. — Replicates reduce variance for stochastic systems. (contested)
- Record all inputs, outputs, tokens, latency, and environment per case. — Full traces make failures diagnosable.
- Snapshot retrieval corpora/reranker models used during eval. — Changing corpora invalidate retrieval comparability.
- Disallow network calls during scoring except approved mocks. — Network variance creates flakiness.

Regression Tracking and Gates
- Always compare against the immediate previous baseline and a long-term baseline. — Dual baselines detect slow drifts and sudden regressions.
- Compute confidence intervals via bootstrap and gate on effect sizes, not just p-values. — Practical significance prevents p-hacking.
- Define acceptance thresholds per metric and per tag before running. — Pre-registration prevents moving goalposts.
- Fail the build on any safety regression regardless of aggregate score. — Safety is a hard constraint.
- Track run metadata: git SHA, dataset/prompt/model versions, config hash, and runner. — Metadata makes results auditable.
- Visualize per-tag and per-criterion deltas with trend lines. — Visualization reveals systemic issues quickly.

Performance and Cost
- Measure p50/p95/p99 latency and average tokens in/out per test and per tag. — Distributional stats capture tail risk.
- Separate cold-start from warm-path timing and report both. — Cold starts dominate user-perceived latency.
- Include retries and tool invocation overheads in latency. — Users experience the whole pipeline, not just model time.
- Record API pricing version and compute cost per test and per suite. — Price changes can mask cost regressions.
- Enforce budget caps per run and fail gracefully when exceeded. — Budgets prevent runaway spend in CI.
- Disable caches during performance runs and mark results as uncached. — Caches hide true latency.
- Run lightweight smoke tests on every commit and full suites on schedule or before release. — Tiered testing balances speed and coverage.

Safety
- Include prompt-injection and jailbreak tests across all evals. — Attacks appear in every domain.
- Test for hallucination and overclaiming using adversarial and null-context cases. — Confident wrong answers are safety issues.
- Scan outputs for PII and sensitive content; fail on threshold breaches. — Data protection is non-negotiable.
- For RAG, verify citations ground claims and links resolve. — Grounding prevents fabricated sources.
- For tools, block exfiltrative actions and validate tool arguments against policies. — Tool misuse amplifies harm.
- Log and review safety failures with reproducible minimal cases. — Triage requires crisp artifacts.

RAG-Specific
- Evaluate retriever recall@k and MRR on labeled queries. — Retrieval quality bounds generation quality.
- Score answer correctness conditional on evidence presence and absence. — Separate retrieval vs generation failure modes.
- Enforce citation presence and coverage; penalize uncited claims. — Citations create accountability.
- Validate chunking and windowing configs; treat them as versioned artifacts. — Preprocessing changes affect recall silently.
- Include domain-shift queries and OOD detection tests. — RAG must fail safe on unknowns.

Agent/Tool Use
- Evaluate plan quality, tool selection accuracy, and argument validity. — Agents fail in planning as much as in execution.
- Mock tools with record–replay for correctness tests and use live tools only in perf/safety sandboxes. — Controlled environments prevent side effects.
- Score end states and key intermediate invariants (e.g., valid SQL, non-destructive ops). — Intermediate checks locate faults earlier.
- Cap tool loop iterations and time; fail on exceeding limits. — Infinite loops and stalls are production failure modes.

Execution and Error Handling
- Treat any unparsable or schema-invalid output as a test failure. — If you can’t parse it, you can’t use it.
- Timebox model and tool calls and record timeouts distinctly. — Timeouts are different from wrong answers.
- Fail fast on harness errors and report separately from model failures. — Harness bugs should not pollute model metrics.
- Re-run only the failed cases to diagnose; never overwrite original artifacts. — Forensics require original evidence.

Style and Documentation
- Use compact, declarative test specs with minimal embedded logic. — Logic in data is hard to maintain.
- Co-locate rubrics with datasets and link to examples of good/bad outputs. — Proximity aids contributors.
- Include a CONTRIBUTING guide for adding/editing tests and labels. — Governance maintains quality as teams grow.

Bias and Fairness
- Stratify by demographic or sensitive attributes where applicable and report disaggregated metrics. — Aggregates can hide harms.
- Exclude sensitive attributes from prompts unless testing fairness specifically. — Avoid inducing bias during eval.
- Periodically rotate adversarial bias tests and re-validate labels with domain experts. — Bias tests stale quickly.

Caching and Artifacts
- Cache model outputs keyed by full prompt+params for local dev only. — Dev caches speed iteration without corrupting CI.
- Store run artifacts (predictions, judgments, traces) in immutable storage. — Immutable logs enable audits and re-analysis.

Security and Privacy
- Redact secrets from prompts, outputs, and logs automatically. — Secrets often leak into traces.
- Limit judge/context visibility to least privilege required. — Principle of least privilege reduces breach impact.
- Prohibit uploading proprietary test data to third-party judges without DPA. — Compliance must be enforced.

Contested Practices
- Prefer pairwise judge comparisons over scalar scores for semantic quality. — Pairwise is often more sensitive but heavier to operate. (contested)
- Ban chain-of-thought in both model and judge during eval. — CoT can contaminate and is hard to standardize. (contested)
- Use temperature=0 for all evals. — Determinism simplifies CI but may misrepresent production variance. (contested)
- Use majority-vote over n samples to score stochastic production. — Better reflects reality but costs more and complicates gating. (contested)

Reporting
- Produce per-run HTML/JSON reports with metrics, CIs, deltas, and top failures. — Clear reports drive action.
- Highlight cost/latency regressions alongside quality changes. — Performance is part of quality.
- Surface the smallest passing change that regresses safety as a blocker. — Safety exceptions must be explicit and rare.

Governance
- Require review for any change to datasets, rubrics, or gates. — Reviews prevent silent metric gaming.
- Archive and deprecate tests via documented RFCs rather than deletion. — Institutional memory avoids repeated mistakes.
- Audit suites quarterly for drift, leakage, and representativeness. — Periodic hygiene keeps evals relevant.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

Creating quality LLM Evaluation Suites involves careful consideration of various principles and trade-offs. Here are some foundational aspects:

1. **Readability**: Evaluation tests should be easy to read and understand. Clear and straightforward prompts and instructions facilitate quicker onboarding and collaboration among engineers and researchers. Ambiguity breeds confusion and misinterpretation, which can lead to flawed evaluations.

2. **Maintainability**: Evaluation code should be modular and well-structured. This enables easy updates and extensions, allowing practitioners to incorporate new test cases or modify existing ones without extensive rework. Maintainable code supports long-term projects by adapting to changing requirements or emerging best practices.

3. **Correctness**: The accuracy of test cases is paramount. Evaluators must ensure that prompts correctly capture the nuances of what they intend to assess. Incorrect evaluations can lead to misjudging model performance, so rigorous validation of each test is vital.

4. **Safety**: LLM applications can produce harmful or biased outputs. The evaluation suite should include safeguards to detect and prevent the propagation of unsafe content. Evaluators must implement mechanisms to scrutinize outputs for ethical considerations and ensure alignment with safety guidelines.

5. **Performance**: Evaluation suites should be efficient, minimizing latency and resource usage. Performance is critical, especially when scaling evaluations across multiple models or configurations. Striking a balance between thoroughness and performance ensures the suite can be utilized effectively without taxing resources unduly.

6. **Common Failure Modes**: Some common pitfalls include designing evaluation metrics that don’t align with user needs or real-world applications, overlooking edge cases in prompts, and failing to consider the diversity of model outputs. Practitioners should regularly review and update the tests to address new failure modes as models evolve.

7. **Scoring Methods**: There is ongoing debate regarding the most effective scoring methods—whether to use exact matches, rubric-based evaluations, or utilize models as judges. Each method has strengths and weaknesses. Practitioners must carefully choose the most suitable method for each test case but should also be aware of their respective biases and limitations.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

# Preamble
This rules file defines best practices for creating LLM Evaluation Suites, aimed at engineers and AI coding assistants. It provides actionable and specific guidelines to ensure effective evaluation of LLM applications across various domains.

## Structure
- Do keep test cases modular. This enhances maintainability and reusability.
- Don’t create tightly-coupled tests. Coupling can lead to cascading failures and hinder updates.

## Readability
- Do use descriptive names for prompts and tests. Clear naming aids in understanding their purpose.
- Don’t use jargon without definition. This can confuse those unfamiliar with specific terms.

## Maintainability
- Do document all test cases with brief descriptions. Documentation aids future modifications and understanding.
- Don’t neglect version control on evaluation code. It prevents loss of critical changes and allows traceability.

## Correctness
- Do validate test outputs against expected results rigorously. This ensures reliability in performance assessments.
- Don’t ignore edge cases in prompts. Overlooking them can result in unanticipated failures during evaluation.

## Safety
- Do implement checks for harmful or biased content in outputs. This safeguards against unethical use of LLMs.
- Don’t allow unfiltered outputs to be evaluated. Unchecked results can propagate inaccuracies and biases.

## Performance
- Do optimize prompts for response time and efficiency. Performance matters, especially in large-scale evaluations.
- Don’t sacrifice completeness for speed. While efficiency is important, a comprehensive evaluation is paramount.

## Scoring Methods
- Do use diverse scoring methods to evaluate different aspects of model performance. A multi-faceted approach provides a holistic view.
- Don’t rely solely on exact match scores. This can overlook nuanced performance differences that are critical for evaluation.

## Regression Tracking
- Do implement automated regression tracking for evaluation results. This helps identify issues after model updates.
- Don’t ignore historical data trends. Learning from past evaluations informs better future testing practices.

## Contestation 
- (contested) Do prioritize rubric-based evaluations over exact match scores when assessing qualitative aspect. Some practitioners argue all methods provide valuable insights; however, rubric-based assessments capture subtleties better.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

LLM evaluation suites are not unit tests. They are statistical instruments measuring a noisy, non-deterministic system against fuzzy specifications. Good suites treat this reality head-on instead of pretending LLMs are deterministic functions.

**Readability.** An eval suite is a contract between product, research, and engineering about what the system *should* do. If a PM can't read a test case and understand the expected behavior, the suite has failed as documentation. Cases should be concrete, self-contained, and named by the capability they probe ("refuses_medical_diagnosis", not "test_7"). Rubrics must be written in plain language and version-controlled next to the dataset.

**Maintainability.** Datasets rot. Models change. Rubrics drift. The worst failure mode is a suite that runs green because everyone stopped trusting it. Maintainability means: datasets are versioned (not mutated in place), scoring code is separated from dataset content, every example has provenance, and "golden" outputs are regenerated through an auditable process — never hand-edited in the CI artifact. Splitting datasets into **smoke** (fast, every PR), **regression** (nightly), and **exploratory** (on-demand, expensive) is essential; conflating them guarantees either slow CI or skipped evals.

**Correctness.** This is where most suites fail. Exact-match is correct only when the output space is genuinely constrained (classification, JSON fields, tool call names). For free-form text, exact-match is theater. Rubric-based scoring and LLM-as-judge are the realistic options, but both have failure modes:

- LLM-judges exhibit position bias, length bias, self-preference bias, and sycophancy. They must be calibrated against human labels on a holdout set, and calibration must be re-measured when you change judge models. Anyone claiming >90% judge agreement without a human-labeled calibration set is bluffing.
- Rubrics must be *binary and decomposed*. A single 1–5 Likert score is nearly useless; five binary checks ("cites a source", "answers the question", "no hallucinated entities", etc.) is auditable and debuggable.
- Pairwise comparison is more reliable than absolute scoring, but doesn't give you an absolute quality number — you need both.

**Regression tracking.** Single-run pass/fail is insufficient because LLMs are stochastic. Either pin `temperature=0` and `seed` (and accept that you're measuring one point in distribution space), or run N samples and track mean + variance with confidence intervals. Treat score deltas smaller than the run-to-run variance as noise. Store every run's raw outputs, not just scores — you'll need them when a metric moves and no one knows why.

**Safety.** Safety evals are adversarial and separate from capability evals. They need red-team cases that are *actually* adversarial (not polite requests for contraband), and they should include prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data exfiltration attempts for agents/RAG. Safety regressions should block releases; capability regressions should require review.

**Performance.** Cost and latency are first-class metrics, not afterthoughts. Track p50/p95/p99 latency and per-example token cost on every run. A 2% quality gain at 3x cost is usually a loss. Instrument at the span level (LLM call, tool call, retrieval) so you can attribute regressions.

**Common failure modes.**
- Dataset leakage into training data (especially for public benchmarks — use private holdouts).
- Judge-model == generator-model (self-preference bias inflates scores).
- Over-fitting prompts to the eval set ("prompt-hacking the benchmark").
- Flaky tests silently retried until green.
- Evals that only cover happy paths; no adversarial, ambiguous, or out-of-distribution cases.
- Treating aggregate scores as the product; ignoring per-slice performance (a model can improve on average while regressing on your most important user segment).

**Contested areas.** Reasonable practitioners disagree on: (a) whether LLM-as-judge is trustworthy enough for release gating, (b) whether to pin `temperature=0` or sample, (c) how much synthetic data belongs in eval sets, (d) whether pairwise or absolute scoring is the right default, and (e) whether to use the same model family as judge and generator. I take positions on these below.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

```markdown
# LLM Evaluation Suite Rules

**Scope:** Evaluation suites for LLM-backed products — prompts, agents, RAG pipelines.
**Audience:** Engineers, ML practitioners, and AI coding assistants building or modifying eval code and datasets.

These rules assume you ship LLM features to users and need defensible, reproducible quality signals.

---

## Dataset Design

- **Version datasets immutably.** Treat each dataset as a content-addressed artifact; never mutate in place. — Enables reproducible historical comparisons.
- **Split into smoke, regression, and exploratory tiers.** Smoke runs on every PR (<2 min), regression nightly, exploratory on-demand. — Prevents CI slowdown and skipped evals.
- **Record provenance for every example.** Source, author, date, and reason for inclusion. — Lets you audit and prune stale cases.
- **Include adversarial, ambiguous, and OOD cases explicitly.** Label them as such. — Happy-path-only suites give false confidence.
- **Maintain a private holdout that never leaves your infra.** — Public benchmarks leak into training data.
- **Stratify by user-relevant slices (intent, language, user tier, document type).** Report per-slice scores, not just aggregates. — Aggregate wins often hide slice regressions.
- **Cap synthetic data at a known fraction and label it.** Never let synthetic examples silently dominate. (contested) — Synthetic data amplifies model biases into the eval.
- **Write each case to test one capability.** — Multi-purpose cases make failures ambiguous.

## Scoring Methods

- **Use exact-match only for constrained outputs (labels, JSON fields, tool names).** — It's meaningless for free-form text.
- **Decompose rubrics into binary checks.** Five yes/no criteria beats one 1–5 score. — Binary checks are auditable and debuggable.
- **Calibrate every LLM-judge against human labels on a holdout.** Report agreement (Cohen's κ or similar) and re-calibrate when the judge model changes. — Uncalibrated judges are vibes.
- **Never use the same model family as judge and generator for release-gating evals.** — Self-preference bias inflates scores by 5–15%.
- **Randomize position and ordering in pairwise judge prompts.** — LLM judges have strong position bias.
- **Use pairwise comparison for model-vs-model, absolute rubrics for regression tracking.** — Each answers a different question.
- **Require chain-of-thought from judges and log it.** — Judge reasoning is your debug trail when scores move.
- **Reject LLM-as-judge as the sole release gate for safety-critical behavior.** Use human review or deterministic checks. (contested) — Judges fail silently on exactly the cases that matter most.

## Regression Tracking

- **Pin model version, temperature, seed, and prompt hash for every run.** — Without pinning, you can't attribute deltas.
- **Run N≥5 samples per case for stochastic configs and report mean + 95% CI.** — Single runs are noise.
- **Treat score deltas smaller than run-to-run variance as noise, not signal.** — Chasing noise wastes weeks.
- **Store raw outputs, not just scores, for every run.** — You will need them to diagnose regressions.
- **Block merges on safety regressions; require review on capability regressions.** — Not all regressions are equal.
- **Alert on per-slice regressions, not only aggregate.** — Aggregates hide the failures users notice.
- **Prefer temperature=0 with seed pinning for regression suites; sample for capability evals.** (contested) — Deterministic runs catch regressions faster; sampling measures real user experience.

## Agents & RAG

- **Evaluate retrieval and generation separately before evaluating end-to-end.** — Composite scores hide which stage regressed.
- **Measure retrieval with recall@k and MRR against labeled relevant docs.** — "The LLM answered well" isn't a retrieval metric.
- **Include cases where retrieval returns nothing or returns adversarial chunks.** — Real corpora are messy.
- **For agents, evaluate per-step decisions (tool choice, args) in addition to final outcome.** — End-to-end success masks broken intermediate reasoning.
- **Include prompt injection and data exfiltration cases for any agent with tools.** — These are the real production failures.

## Cost & Latency

- **Track p50/p95/p99 latency and per-example token cost on every run.** — Quality-only evals hide 3x cost regressions.
- **Instrument at the span level (LLM call, tool call, retrieval).** — Attribution is impossible without spans.
- **Define a cost budget per eval case and fail the run if exceeded.** — Budgets prevent silent cost explosions.
- **Report quality-per-dollar, not just quality.** — Pareto trade-offs are the actual decision.

## Structure & Code

- **Separate dataset content (data) from scoring logic (code) from rubrics (text).** — Mixing them blocks reuse and review.
- **Make every eval runnable locally on a subset with one command.** — If devs can't run it, they won't fix it.
- **Log every judge prompt, response, and score to a queryable store.** — Spreadsheets don't scale past 50 cases.
- **Name cases by capability, not by number.** `refuses_medical_diagnosis` > `case_042`. — Names are documentation.
- **Fail loudly on API errors; never silently retry into a pass.** — Flaky green is worse than red.

## Anti-Patterns (Don't Do These)

- **Don't tune prompts against the same eval set you report scores on.** — That's overfitting; keep a blind holdout.
- **Don't aggregate scores across incommensurable rubrics into a single headline number.** — It's uninterpretable and easy to game.
- **Don't use a 1–5 Likert from an LLM judge as a primary metric.** — Judges cluster on 3 and 4; signal is weak.
- **Don't delete failing cases because they're "unfair."** Mark them known-hard and track them. — Deletion is how quality silently erodes.
- **Don't ship an eval suite without a human-labeled calibration set, however small.** — Ungrounded metrics drift undetected.
```

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

### Core Principles

LLM evaluation suites sit at a critical juncture: they are simultaneously **measurement instruments** (they must be valid and reliable) and **living documentation** (they encode what product quality means). This dual nature creates tension.

**Readability and Maintainability** are foundational. An evaluation suite that engineers cannot understand will not be trusted, extended, or fixed. This means:
- Test cases must be self-documenting; the intent should be clear without external context.
- Scoring logic must be explicit and auditable. Hidden complexity (especially in LLM-as-judge prompts) degrades confidence.
- Datasets and thresholds should live close to the test code, versioned together, not scattered across spreadsheets.

**Correctness** is the hardest problem. LLM outputs are inherently variable, which creates several challenges:

1. **Ground truth is often ambiguous.** Unlike unit tests where correctness is binary, many LLM tasks (summarization, helpfulness, tone) are subjective. You must acknowledge this and design rubrics accordingly. Pretending evaluation is objective when it isn't is a trap.

2. **Scoring methods introduce systematic bias.** Exact-match scoring misses valid paraphrases. Rubric-based scoring is labor-intensive but more reliable than LLM-as-judge for critical tasks. LLM-as-judge is fast and scales, but it's unstable across model versions and can be gamed by prompt engineering. There is no silver bullet.

3. **Sample size matters.** A 10-example test suite will show noise, not signal. The cost of generation and labeling pushes people toward small datasets, but this is penny-wise and pound-foolish. At minimum: 50 examples per category; 200+ for production pipelines.

4. **Temporal validity is brittle.** A test that passes today may fail tomorrow when:
   - The underlying LLM is updated or replaced.
   - The API behavior changes (temperature, system prompt, context window).
   - External data shifts (if evaluating a RAG system, the corpus changed).
   
   Plan for regression tracking and understand baseline drift.

**Safety** surfaces in two ways:

1. **Evaluation-driven degradation.** If engineers optimize for test scores, the suite becomes a perverse incentive. This happens when rubrics reward easily-gamed signals (like length, keyword matching) over real quality. Good rubrics align with user experience, not implementation details.

2. **Cost and latency leakage.** Evaluation suites can become expensive and slow. Running a 500-example suite with LLM-as-judge evaluation across 3 model variants is $50+ and 10+ minutes. Engineers will skip it, making it useless. Instrument cost and latency explicitly. Set budgets.

**Performance** is contextual:

- **Latency:** Evaluation suites must run in developer feedback loops (~seconds to minutes). If they take 30+ minutes, they won't be run locally and will only catch issues at CI time. Stratify tests (fast smoke tests, slow regression suite) accordingly.
- **Cost:** Unbounded evaluation costs destroy adoption. Set cost budgets per suite per run. Cache evaluations where possible (same input + same scoring method should be idempotent).
- **Repeatability:** LLM outputs are stochastic. A single-run evaluation is a single sample. For critical decisions, run multiple times and report variance, not just a point estimate.

### Common Failure Modes

1. **Conflating proxy metrics with product metrics.** A high BLEU score or rubric score doesn't guarantee users are happy. Regularly audit against real user feedback.

2. **Test set memorization.** If the LLM sees test examples during training, evaluation becomes unreliable. Use held-out, fresh datasets for regression testing.

3. **Rubric drift.** Rubrics written in natural language are subjective. Without calibration, different annotators (or the same annotator over time) score inconsistently. Use inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) to validate rubrics before deploying them.

4. **LLM-as-judge instability.** A judge prompt that works with GPT-4 may fail with Claude or a smaller model. The judge itself can be biased (e.g., favoring verbose outputs, agreeing with system prompts). Validate judges against ground truth and across model boundaries.

5. **Ignoring cost and latency.** A comprehensive evaluation suite that no one runs is worse than no suite. Make cost and latency first-class concerns, not afterthoughts.

6. **Silent failures.** If an evaluation crashes on a single example (malformed JSON, API timeout), the entire run can be silently corrupted. Defensive coding is critical.

### Contested Areas

**Contested: How much of evaluation should be automated vs. manual?**
- Pragmatists argue: automate what's reliable (exact match, simple rubrics), keep human-in-the-loop for subjective cases.
- Efficiency advocates argue: build good LLM-as-judge systems and let them scale.
- Quality advocates argue: only human evaluation is trustworthy; automation is for rough signal.
- **My position:** Start with hybrid. Use rubric-based scoring for 80% of cases (sufficient signal), reserve human review for edge cases and calibration. LLM-as-judge is useful for rapid iteration, but validate it against ground truth before promoting to production.

**Contested: Should evaluation thresholds be fixed or adaptive?**
- Static thresholds are deterministic and auditable.
- Adaptive thresholds (e.g., "pass if better than baseline by 2%") reduce brittleness but obscure what "good" actually means.
- **My position:** Use fixed thresholds for public-facing products; they are a commitment. Use adaptive thresholds in development. Track both.

**Contested: Who owns evaluation suites?**
- Product teams argue: they know what users care about.
- ML/AI teams argue: they know what's technically feasible and how to measure it.
- Engineering teams argue: they need to maintain it.
- **My position:** Evaluation suites must be owned by whoever ships the product. Input should be collaborative, but final decision authority belongs with the people responsible for quality.

---
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

### Scope and Audience

This rules file defines standards for **LLM Evaluation Suites**: test collections that measure the quality of LLM applications (prompts, agents, RAG systems). It is written for engineers and AI coding assistants who design, build, and maintain these suites. Rules are organized by concern and are prescriptive, not advisory.

---

### Structure & Organization

**Do organize tests into categories (happy path, edge cases, regression, safety).** Clear categorization makes it obvious what's being tested and enables selective execution (smoke tests in dev, full suite in CI).

**Do co-locate test data, scoring logic, and thresholds with test code.** Don't scatter them across spreadsheets, databases, or separate config files. Version control is your friend.

**Do use YAML, JSON, or a structured format for test cases, not free-form text.** Automation and tooling must be able to parse and version-diff your tests.

**Do name test cases descriptively.** Instead of `test_01`, use `test_rag_query_retrieval_empty_corpus` — the name should tell you what's being tested and the condition.

**Do include metadata in each test case: author, date added, category, and (if applicable) a link to the issue or feature it validates.** This enables triage and ownership.

**Do version your test suite separately from your application code if the test suite outlives a single product iteration.** (Contested: shared vs. separate versioning; I favor separate because evaluation expectations evolve differently than code.)

---

### Dataset Design

**Do use at least 50 examples per test category; 200+ for production-critical paths.** Small datasets (<20 examples) produce noisy signals and are statistically unreliable.

**Do use held-out, fresh test data that was never part of training data or prompt engineering iterations.** Memorization invalidates evaluation.

**Do ensure test data reflects real-world distribution and edge cases.** If 80% of user queries are short, 80% of your test queries should be short; the remaining 20% should probe long/complex queries.

**Do include adversarial or boundary-case examples: empty inputs, very long inputs, unusual formatting, ambiguous intent.** Smooth performance on typical cases masks brittle systems.

**Do document the source and generation method for each test set.** (E.g., "pulled from production logs Jan–Mar 2024," "synthesized via few-shot generation and manually curated," "from public benchmark X.") Reproducibility matters.

**Do track and publish inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa, Fleiss' kappa, Krippendorff's alpha) for any human-annotated labels.** If annotators don't agree, the ground truth is unreliable, and your evaluation is suspect.

**Do not use the same test set for both development and final evaluation.** Split into dev (for tuning), validation (for threshold selection), and held-out test (for final reporting).

---

### Scoring Methods & Rubrics

**Do define rubrics explicitly in prose, with concrete examples for each score level.** A rubric that says "score 1–5 based on quality" is useless. One that says "score 5 if the summary covers all three key facts and introduces no hallucinations; score 3 if it covers two facts or introduces one minor hallucination" is actionable.

**Do use exact-match scoring only for tasks where exact match is truly correct (e.g., multiple-choice with one right answer, SQL queries that return identical result sets).** For open-ended tasks, it will underestimate true performance.

**Do prefer rubric-based scoring for subjective dimensions (tone, relevance, completeness).** Rubrics are labor-intensive but human-interpretable and stable.

**Do use LLM-as-judge scoring for rapid iteration and scale, but validate it against ground truth before trusting it.** Run side-by-side comparisons: judge scores vs. human scores on the same examples. Report agreement. (Contested: I accept LLM judges; some practitioners distrust them entirely. Use them with eyes open.)

**Do parameterize judge prompts and log them verbatim in results.** If you change the judge prompt, evaluation results are not comparable. Version your judges like you version your models.

**Do run each evaluation multiple times (≥3) with the same parameters and report the mean and variance of the score.** Stochasticity matters; a single run is a single sample, not ground truth.

**Do not use string similarity (BLEU, ROUGE) as a primary metric for open-ended generation tasks.** These metrics correlate poorly with user satisfaction and are gamed by surface-level manipulation.

**Do report not just aggregate scores but also per-example results and failure modes.** Aggregates hide systematic errors. If 90% of failures are on a specific category, that's actionable; "accuracy is 87%" is not.

---

### Regression Tracking & Baseline Management

**Do establish a baseline (the current production model's performance) before making changes.** You cannot know if a change is an improvement without a reference point.

**Do track results over time in a queryable format (database, artifact, or dedicated tooling).** This enables trend analysis and detection of gradual degradation.

**Do alert on performance regressions and understand what caused them before deploying to production.** A regression in accuracy or latency that reaches production is a process failure, not an LLM problem.

**Do not accept regressions in critical metrics without explicit justification and sign-off.** Create a policy: e.g., "Regressions > 2% in accuracy require approval from the product owner."

**Do include a "no change" baseline: run the same evaluation twice on the same model with identical parameters.** If you see variance, you have a problem with reproducibility that matters more than the actual score.

**Do track model version, temperature, system prompt, context length, and any other parameter that could affect output in evaluation metadata.** Changes to these are implicit regressions if not tracked.

---

### LLM-as-Judge Evaluation

**Do validate judge prompts against human annotations on a held-out calibration set before using them in regression testing.** Report agreement metrics (exact match, within-1 score, rank correlation).

**Do test judge prompts across different model families (GPT, Claude, Llama, etc.) and report variance.** A judge optimized for GPT-4 may behave differently with Claude 3.

**Do detect and document cases where the judge disagrees with ground truth, and investigate root causes.** Edge cases where judges fail are data sources for rubric refinement.

**Do not use a single judge instance for large evaluation runs.** Parallelize judges and ensure each evaluation call is independent and idempotent (same input → same score, given fixed seed/temperature).

**Do include instructions in judge prompts to output structured JSON with a score and reasoning.** Structured output is easier to parse and analyze than free-form text.

**Do expose judge reasoning in evaluation reports, not just scores.** If a judge scored low, why? This context is essential for debugging.

---

### Error Handling & Robustness

**Do implement timeouts for all evaluation operations (LLM calls, database queries, scoring logic).** Unbounded operations will eventually hang, corrupting the entire evaluation run.

**Do catch and log all exceptions without silently swallowing them.** A malformed JSON response or API error on example 47 of 500 should fail the run loudly, not be ignored.

**Do implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures (API rate limits, network timeouts).** Give up after N retries and report the failure, don't retry forever.

**Do validate outputs before scoring.** If an LLM response is malformed (truncated, invalid JSON), report it as a failure and include it in metrics. Don't skip the example.

**Do make evaluation deterministic where possible.** Use fixed random seeds, pinned model versions, and frozen test data. Flaky tests erode confidence.

**Do write evaluation code with the assumption that it will be run thousands of times.** Defensive coding (input validation, bounds checking, explicit error messages) pays off.

---

### Cost & Latency Instrumentation

**Do measure and log the cost (in dollars, not just tokens) of every evaluation run.** Make cost first-class. If evaluation costs $100 per run, engineers need to know it.

**Do measure and log the latency of each evaluation step: data loading, LLM calls, scoring, aggregation.** Break down where time is spent.

**Do set cost and latency budgets for your evaluation suite and alert if they are exceeded.** If smoke tests should take <1 minute, fail if they take 5 minutes.

**Do cache evaluation results keyed on (input hash, model version, scoring method, parameters).** Evaluating the same example twice should reuse the cached result, not re-run the LLM.

**Do report cost and latency alongside accuracy in all evaluation reports.** A 95% accurate solution that costs $1000/run is a different trade-off than a 94% solution that costs $10/run.

**Do stratify tests: fast smoke tests (critical paths, <1 min), medium regression tests (all cases, <10 min), slow deep dives (comprehensive, <1 hour).** Make smoke tests easy to run locally; reserve deeper tests for CI/CD.

---

### Correctness & Validation

**Do not assume ground truth is objective.** If humans disagree on the right answer, your evaluation is measuring inter-rater agreement, not ground truth. Document this.

**Do measure and publish inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa or Krippendorff's alpha) for any hand-labeled data.** If κ < 0.7, the labels are too noisy; improve the rubric and re-label.

**Do audit evaluation results against real-world feedback on a rolling basis.** If the test suite says quality is 90% but users report issues, the suite is not measuring what matters.

**Do include sanity checks: tests that any reasonable model should pass (e.g., "the model outputs text" or "the model doesn't output 'I don't know' for factual queries it should answer").** These catch catastrophic failures early.

**Do not over-fit to the test suite.** Engineers will optimize for test scores; if tests don't align with user experience, they will produce brittle systems. This is a governance problem, not a technical one, but acknowledge it.

**Do review test results with a human (code review style) before promoting changes to production.** Automated gates are necessary but not sufficient.

---

### Documentation & Communication

**Do document what each test category measures and why it matters.** Link tests to product requirements or user feedback where possible.

**Do include a README in your evaluation suite repo explaining: how to run tests, how to interpret results, how to add new tests, and how to update baselines.** New contributors should be able to use the suite without asking questions.

**Do report results in a format readable by humans and machines.** A markdown table is better than raw JSON; a dashboard is better still.

**Do include a summary line in every evaluation report: "Accuracy: 87%, Latency: 2.3s/call, Cost: $45/run, Baseline: 85% (no regression)."** Make the headline immediately obvious.

**Do document decisions: why you chose LLM-as-judge over rubrics, why the threshold is 0.85, why you excluded a category.** Future-you will thank you.

---

### Safety & Bias

**Do evaluate for known failure modes in your domain.** If you're building a RAG system, test for hallucination and retrieval failure explicitly. If you're fine-tuning, test for capability regressions.

**Do include tests for model biases if they are relevant to your product.** Blind spots in evaluation hide systemic problems.

**Do not allow a test to be marked "pass" if it's actually a skip or an exception.** Passing by doing nothing is worse than failing loudly.

**Do document any limitations or assumptions in your evaluation suite.** (E.g., "We only test English queries; multilingual support is not evaluated here.")

**Do review and update your evaluation suite when you encounter a user-reported issue.** Add a test case for it, make sure it fails with the old code and passes with the fix. This is the definition of regression prevention.

---

### Version Control & Reproducibility

**Do commit test data, test code, and scoring logic to version control together.** Don't externalize data.

**Do tag stable evaluation suites (e.g., `eval-v1.0`) and note what changed between versions.** This enables reproducible comparisons across time.

**Do pin all dependencies: model versions, API endpoints, library versions.** Evaluation is brittle; small changes to dependencies can shift results.

**Do store evaluation artifacts (results, logs, intermediate outputs) in version-controlled or archived storage.** You will want to compare results 6 months later; don't lose them.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

An LLM Evaluation Suite is the single most critical piece of infrastructure for developing robust, reliable, and safe AI applications. It is the equivalent of a comprehensive unit, integration, and end-to-end testing framework for traditional software, but adapted for the stochastic and semantic nature of language models. A good suite is not a one-time creation; it is a living system that co-evolves with the application.

The principles below are derived from hard-won experience building and scaling LLM-powered systems. They prioritize long-term velocity, safety, and correctness over short-term convenience.

**Readability and Maintainability**
LLM evaluation is an iterative, experimental process. An unreadable or brittle evaluation suite actively hinders this process. The primary failure mode is mixing data, logic, and configuration into monolithic scripts. When an engineer needs to add a new test case or tweak a scoring rubric, they shouldn't have to refactor Python code.

The core principle is **separation of concerns**. Test case data (prompts, contexts, golden answers) should live in simple, declarative formats (like YAML or JSON) separate from the evaluation execution code. This allows non-engineers to contribute test cases and makes the suite's coverage easy to audit. The logic for running evaluations and scoring results should be modular, reusable, and well-documented.

**Correctness**
Correctness in an LLM eval suite has two dimensions: the correctness of the test data and the correctness of the scoring. A failure in either invalidates the entire result.

1.  **Data Correctness:** The "golden" dataset is your source of truth. If your expected outputs are wrong, ambiguous, or outdated ("dataset rot"), you are optimizing for the wrong thing. Test cases must be specific, unambiguous, and regularly reviewed. Every test case should have a clear author and a description of its intent.

2.  **Scoring Correctness:** A simple "exact match" is rarely sufficient. Scoring logic must accurately reflect the desired quality attributes. A factually correct but rudely-phrased answer should fail a "professional tone" rubric. LLM-as-judge can provide nuanced, semantic scoring, but it introduces its own correctness challenge: bias, inconsistency, and drift. The judge's prompt, model version, and scoring rubric must be version-controlled and validated just like any other piece of code. Relying on an unpinned, latest model for judging is a recipe for non-reproducible results.

**Safety**
An LLM evaluation suite is your primary defense against deploying an unsafe model. Safety is not an emergent property of "good" performance; it must be explicitly and adversarially tested. The suite must contain a dedicated, non-negotiable set of tests for failure modes like prompt injection, jailbreaking, generating harmful content, leaking PII, and succumbing to stereotypical biases. These tests are not about getting the "right" answer; they are about the model refusing to answer or providing a safe, canned response. A failure on a safety test must be a hard blocker for any deployment.

**Performance**
For LLM applications, performance is not just about execution speed. It is a multi-faceted concern:

*   **Latency:** The time-to-first-token and time-to-full-response directly impact user experience. Evals must measure and track this.
*   **Cost:** Every LLM call has a direct monetary cost tied to token count. An otherwise "correct" change that doubles the prompt size can be a business failure. Evals must instrument and report token counts and estimated costs.
*   **Evaluation Suite Performance:** The suite itself must be fast enough to run in a CI/CD pipeline. This creates a trade-off: a 10,000-case dataset is comprehensive but too slow for pre-commit checks. This necessitates a tiered approach: a small, fast "smoke test" suite for CI, and a larger, comprehensive "full" suite for nightly or pre-release builds.

**Common Failure Modes**
*   **Metric Myopia:** Over-optimizing for a single, simple metric (e.g., ROUGE, BLEU, exact match) while regressing on crucial but harder-to-measure qualities like factuality, tone, or helpfulness.
*   **Golden Set Overfitting:** The model becomes exceptionally good at the specific examples in the test set but fails on slight variations, indicating it has "memorized" the test rather than learned the underlying task.
*   **LLM-as-Judge Drift:** The model used for judging is updated by its provider (e.g., `gpt-4` is not static), causing scoring criteria to shift silently over time and invalidating historical comparisons.
*   **Lack of Regression Tracking:** Simply running evals is not enough. You must store, visualize, and alert on the results over time. Without a historical view, you cannot know if a change is an improvement or a regression.

**Contested Areas**
Practitioners disagree on several key points, usually trading scalability and speed for control and accuracy.

*   **LLM-as-Judge vs. Human-in-the-Loop:** LLM-as-judge is fast, scalable, and can score nuanced attributes. However, it can be biased, inconsistent, and its reasoning can be opaque. A human with a detailed rubric is the gold standard for quality but is slow, expensive, and doesn't scale. The pragmatic approach is a hybrid: use LLM-as-judge for large-scale regression testing, but periodically validate a subset of its judgments with human experts.
*   **Synthetic Data Generation:** Using an LLM to generate thousands of test cases is a tempting way to scale a dataset. Proponents argue it helps cover the long tail of inputs. Critics argue it creates a feedback loop where a model's own biases and blind spots are encoded into its test suite, leading to illusory progress. A balanced rule is to use synthetic data for augmenting and diversifying test cases, not as a replacement for human-curated "golden" examples that test core functionality and known failure modes.
*   **Deterministic vs. Stochastic Evaluation:** Running evals with `temperature=0` ensures reproducibility, making it easy to spot regressions. However, it doesn't reflect real-world usage where temperature is > 0. Running with a non-zero temperature measures the *distribution* of model performance but makes pass/fail decisions harder. The best practice is to do both: use `temperature=0` for core CI regression tests and run separate, periodic "variance" tests with a higher temperature.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

### LLM Evaluation Suite Best Practices

This document provides rules for building effective, maintainable, and robust evaluation suites for LLM applications. It is intended for AI engineers and AI coding assistants.

---

### Dataset Design

| Rule                                                                                              | Rationale                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Separate** test case data from evaluation logic.                                                | Rationale: Allows test cases (in YAML/JSON) to be managed independently of code. |
| **Define** a clear, versioned schema for your test case data.                                     | Rationale: Prevents ambiguity and ensures all necessary fields (e.g., metadata) exist. |
| **Include** metadata in each test case.                                                           | Rationale: Fields like `id`, `author`, `intent`, and `tags` are crucial for debugging and slicing. |
| **Create** distinct datasets for distinct tasks.                                                  | Rationale: A single "god dataset" becomes unmanageable; split by capability (e.g., RAG, summarization). |
| **Commit** your datasets to version control.                                                      | Rationale: Treats your evaluation data with the same rigor as your source code.      |
| **(contested) Use** synthetic data generation to augment, not replace, human-curated test cases.  | Rationale: Balances the need for scale with the risk of amplifying model biases. |
| **Curate** a "golden" set of challenging, representative, and adversarial examples.               | Rationale: This small, high-quality set is your most important asset for measuring true progress. |

### Scoring & Metrics

| Rule                                                                                              | Rationale                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Use** a combination of metric types.                                                            | Rationale: Relying on one metric (e.g., exact match) leads to blind spots.             |
| **(contested) Use** LLM-as-judge for nuanced, rubric-based scoring.                                 | Rationale: It's the most scalable way to evaluate semantic qualities like tone or helpfulness. |
| **Pin** the model version for your LLM-as-judge evaluators.                                       | Rationale: Prevents "judge drift" where a new model version changes scoring behavior.    |
| **Version-control** your LLM-as-judge prompts and rubrics.                                        | Rationale: The judge's instructions are a critical part of your test logic.          |
| **Log** the full reasoning from your LLM-as-judge, not just the final score.                       | Rationale: The reasoning is essential for debugging false positives/negatives.       |
| **Define** success criteria as a holistic score, not a single metric.                             | Rationale: A change might improve factuality but harm conciseness; trade-offs must be explicit. |
| **Write** explicit unit tests for your custom scoring functions.                                  | Rationale: Your evaluation code can have bugs too.                                   |

### Structure & Maintainability

| Rule                                                                                             | Rationale                                                                                  |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Structure** the evaluation suite to be runnable from a single CLI command.                     | Rationale: Simplifies execution in local environments and CI/CD pipelines.                 |
| **Implement** tiered evaluation suites.                                                          | Rationale: Use a fast "smoke" suite for CI and a comprehensive "full" suite for nightly builds. |
| **Log** evaluation results to a structured, queryable format (e.g., JSONL).                      | Rationale: Enables programmatic analysis, historical tracking, and dashboarding.         |
| **Store** and version all evaluation run artifacts.                                              | Rationale: Preserves inputs, outputs, scores, and logs for future regression analysis.     |
| **Run** primary regression tests with a temperature of 0.                                        | Rationale: Ensures deterministic outputs for reproducible pass/fail checks.              |
| **Isolate** external dependencies like vector databases or APIs.                                 | Rationale: Use fakes or mocks to ensure evals test your logic, not external service uptime. |

### Safety & Security

| Rule                                                                                            | Rationale                                                                                      |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Create** a dedicated, non-negotiable safety evaluation set.                                   | Rationale: Safety must be tested explicitly and adversarially, not as an afterthought.           |
| **Include** tests for prompt injections, jailbreaking, and adversarial inputs.                  | Rationale: Your suite is the first line of defense against known LLM attack vectors.         |
| **Include** tests for harmful content generation, bias, and PII leakage.                        | Rationale: Proactively measures the model's adherence to safety and privacy policies.      |
| **Fail** the entire evaluation run on any safety test failure.                                  | Rationale: Safety regressions are critical failures that must block deployments.             |

### Performance & Cost

| Rule                                                                                   | Rationale                                                                             |
| -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Instrument** and log cost (token counts) for every evaluation run.                   | Rationale: Cost is a primary constraint; changes that triple cost must be justified.    |
| **Instrument** and log latency for every evaluation run.                               | Rationale: User experience is directly tied to model response time.                   |
| **Set** performance budgets for cost and latency.                                      | Rationale: Defines explicit, testable goals for what "fast enough" and "cheap enough" mean. |
| **Fail** the build if performance budgets are exceeded without explicit approval.      | Rationale: Treats performance regressions with the same seriousness as correctness regressions. |

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

LLM Evaluation Suites are critical for ensuring that large language models (LLMs) perform reliably in real-world applications, such as prompts, agents, and RAG pipelines. Good evaluation suites go beyond simple testing; they provide a structured, repeatable process to assess correctness, efficiency, and safety while adapting to the evolving landscape of AI development. Below, I outline the principles, trade-offs, and reasoning for what makes these suites effective, focusing on the required areas: readability, maintainability, correctness, safety, performance, and common failure modes. I'll also highlight contested areas where practitioners disagree, drawing from my expertise in technical best practices.

**Readability** is foundational because evaluation suites are collaborative tools used by engineers, data scientists, and AI assistants. Clear, well-documented code and configurations make it easier to understand intent, reproduce results, and onboard new team members. For instance, using descriptive variable names and modular scripts enhances readability, reducing the cognitive load during reviews. However, there's a trade-off: prioritizing readability might increase initial development time, as engineers balance concise code with explanatory comments.

**Maintainability** ensures suites remain useful as LLMs and applications evolve. Suites should be designed for easy updates, such as swapping datasets or scoring methods without overhauling the entire system. This involves using version-controlled components and abstraction layers for dataset design or scoring logic. Trade-offs arise in regression tracking, where comprehensive historical data storage can bloat storage costs, forcing a decision between detailed tracking and resource efficiency. In contested areas, some practitioners argue for fully automated suites (e.g., using CI/CD pipelines), while others prefer human oversight to handle edge cases, as automation can miss subtle model drifts.

**Correctness** is non-negotiable, as it directly impacts the reliability of LLM evaluations. Suites must accurately measure performance through robust dataset design (e.g., representative samples covering edge cases) and precise scoring methods (e.g., exact match for factual accuracy or rubric-based for nuanced responses). Trade-offs include the choice between automated methods like LLM-as-judge, which is scalable but prone to biases, and human evaluation, which is more accurate but slower and costlier. A common failure mode is "eval suite overfitting," where models are tuned specifically to pass tests, leading to poor generalization—practitioners disagree on mitigation strategies, with some favoring adversarial testing and others relying on synthetic data augmentation.

**Safety** addresses risks like generating harmful outputs or amplifying biases, especially in applications like RAG pipelines. Suites should include checks for toxicity, fairness, and ethical alignment, such as red-teaming prompts. Trade-offs involve balancing safety with performance; overly strict safety checks can flag benign outputs, increasing false positives and slowing evaluations. Contested areas include the use of LLMs for self-evaluation (e.g., LLM-as-judge for safety), where some experts believe it introduces circular reasoning and potential hallucinations, while others see it as a pragmatic, cost-effective approach if properly calibrated.

**Performance** refers to the efficiency of the suite itself, including cost and latency instrumentation. Suites should track metrics like query times, API costs, and resource usage to ensure evaluations are feasible for frequent runs. Trade-offs include optimizing for speed (e.g., using lightweight scoring) versus accuracy (e.g., detailed rubric-based evaluations), which might require more compute. A key failure mode is "silent performance degradation," where suites fail to detect increased latency in RAG pipelines, leading to production issues—here, practitioners disagree on thresholds for alerts, with some advocating aggressive monitoring and others prioritizing flexibility to avoid alert fatigue.

Common failure modes in LLM Evaluation Suites include inconsistent scoring due to poorly defined rubrics, dataset biases that don't reflect real-world diversity, and inadequate regression tracking that misses subtle regressions. For example, relying solely on exact match scoring can overlook semantic nuances, while over-reliance on LLM-as-judge might propagate model errors. In contested spaces, the debate around dataset design—such as using synthetic versus real-world data—highlights disagreements: synthetic data is cheaper and scalable but can lack authenticity, whereas real-world data is more representative but harder to curate ethically.

Overall, the reasoning for good LLM Evaluation Suites centers on creating a balanced framework that prioritizes reliability and adaptability. My opinion is that suites should be opinionated toward automation where possible (e.g., for scoring and tracking) to enable rapid iteration, but with safeguards like human reviews for high-stakes areas, acknowledging that trade-offs require context-specific decisions.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

This rules file outlines best practices for designing and implementing LLM Evaluation Suites, focusing on dataset design, scoring methods, regression tracking, and cost/latency instrumentation. It is intended for engineers and AI coding assistants to ensure suites are readable, maintainable, correct, safe, and performant.

### Structure
- Do organize your evaluation suite into modular components, such as separate modules for datasets, scoring, and tracking. Rationale: Modularity simplifies maintenance and allows independent updates.
- Do use a configuration file (e.g., YAML or JSON) to define suite parameters. Rationale: This centralizes changes and improves readability for team collaboration.
- Don't hardcode dataset paths or scoring thresholds; use variables instead. Rationale: This enhances portability and reduces errors when adapting to new environments.

### Dataset Design
- Do include diverse examples in your datasets, covering edge cases, biases, and real-world scenarios. Rationale: Diversity ensures evaluations reflect actual LLM performance and prevents overfitting.
- Do annotate datasets with metadata for context, such as prompt intent or expected outcomes. Rationale: Metadata improves correctness by enabling targeted analysis and debugging.
- Don't rely solely on synthetic data for critical evaluations (contested). Rationale: Synthetic data is efficient but may not capture real-world complexities, potentially leading to inaccurate results—use it as a supplement.

### Scoring Methods
- Do prioritize rubric-based scoring over exact match for nuanced evaluations, like agent responses. Rationale: Rubrics provide more accurate assessments of semantic quality and context.
- Do implement LLM-as-judge as a default for scalable scoring, with human oversight for high-stakes cases (contested). Rationale: It's faster and cost-effective but can introduce biases, so combine with validation.
- Don't use a single scoring method across all tests; tailor it to the application (e.g., exact match for factual prompts). Rationale: This ensures correctness by matching evaluation to the task's requirements.

### Regression Tracking
- Do integrate automated regression tracking with version control, logging changes in model performance over time. Rationale: This maintains historical context for detecting drifts and ensuring maintainability.
- Do set clear thresholds for regression alerts, such as 5% drops in accuracy. Rationale: Thresholds enable proactive fixes and improve performance by flagging issues early.
- Don't manually review regressions without tooling; automate reports instead. Rationale: Automation reduces errors and supports frequent iterations.

### Safety
- Do include safety checks in every evaluation run, such as toxicity detection for prompts and outputs. Rationale: This prevents harmful behaviors and ensures ethical LLM deployment.
- Do test for bias amplification in RAG pipelines by including diverse demographic examples. Rationale: Bias checks enhance correctness and protect against real-world risks.
- Don't skip safety evaluations for non-production tests; treat them as mandatory. Rationale: Early detection of issues improves overall safety and avoids downstream failures.

### Performance
- Do instrument your suite to track cost and latency metrics for each evaluation run. Rationale: This optimizes resource use and ensures the suite runs efficiently without delays.
- Do cap evaluation runs at a fixed budget, such as limiting API calls per test. Rationale: Budget caps prevent runaway costs and maintain performance in iterative development.
- Don't overlook latency in scoring methods; choose lightweight options like exact match for fast feedback. Rationale: High-latency evaluations hinder frequent testing and regression detection.

### Error Handling
- Do implement robust error handling, such as retries for API failures in LLM-as-judge. Rationale: This ensures reliability and prevents incomplete evaluations.
- Do log all errors with detailed context, including timestamps and inputs. Rationale: Detailed logs aid in debugging and maintain correctness over time.
- Don't propagate errors silently; always raise exceptions or alerts. Rationale: Explicit handling improves safety by making issues visible immediately.

### Style
- Do follow a consistent naming convention, such as snake_case for functions and variables. Rationale: Consistency enhances readability and makes code easier to scan.
- Do document each rule or function with a brief description and rationale. Rationale: Documentation supports maintainability by helping others understand decisions quickly.
- Don't use overly complex code structures; keep functions short and focused. Rationale: Simplicity boosts performance and reduces common failure modes like bugs.
